perturbation.original_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below shall be submitted to arbitration in accordance with The State of California Code of Procedure. The written determination of the arbitration shall be final, binding, and conclusive on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: It is agreed that any disputes that result between the parties whose signatures appear below shall be submitted to arbitration. The written determination of the arbitration shall not be subject to judicial review, modification, or appeal, and the parties expressly waive any rights to such review under California law.
perturbation.explanation: By waiving judicial review, the clause attempts to override California Code of Civil Procedure sections that allow for vacating or correcting arbitration awards under certain circumstances, creating a conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1286.2 and § 1286.6 provide grounds for vacating or correcting an arbitration award. The modified text, by waiving any rights to judicial review, directly contradicts these provisions, which allow a court to vacate an award if, for example, it was procured by corruption, fraud, or other undue means, or if the arbitrators exceeded their powers.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California law by waiving rights to judicial review of arbitration decisions, which are protected under California Code of Civil Procedure § 1286.2 and § 1286.6. The original text defers to the 'State of California Code of Procedure,' which includes the right to judicial review. The modified text's waiver attempts to override these provisions, creating a conflict as per the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'CCP Code of Civil Procedure,' which would include the sections that the modification attempts to circumvent.

perturbation.original_text: If either party sues the other party to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall, in addition to all other damages, be entitled to recover any and all legal fees incurred.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party sues the other party to enforce any of the terms of this agreement and the claim is less than $10,000, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover any and all legal fees incurred.
perturbation.explanation: This modification could violate California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1033, which limits the recovery of costs, including attorney fees, in certain limited civil cases if the judgment could have been rendered in a court of lesser jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1033 governs the recovery of costs in civil actions. Specifically, if a case is brought in a superior court but could have been brought in a court with a lesser jurisdiction (e.g., small claims), the prevailing party's recovery of costs, including attorney's fees, may be limited. The modified text potentially violates this law by mandating the recovery of all legal fees in cases under $10,000 regardless of whether the case could have been brought in a lower court and whether such recovery would be permissible under Section 1033.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1033 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033. (a) Costs or any portion of claimed costs shall be as determined by the court in its discretion in a case other than a limited civil case in accordance with Section 1034 where the prevailing party recovers a judgment that could have been rendered in a limited civil case. (b) When a prevailing plaintiff in a limited civil case recovers less than the amount prescribed by law as the maximum limitation upon the jurisdiction of the small claims court, the following shall apply: (1) When the party could have brought the action in the small claims division but did not do so, the court may, in its discretion, allow or deny costs to the prevailing party, or may allow costs in part in any amount as it deems proper. (2) When the party could not have brought the action in the small claims court, costs and necessary disbursements shall be limited to the actual cost of the filing fee, the actual cost of service of process, and, when otherwise specifically allowed by law, reasonable attorneys’ fees. However, those costs shall only be awarded to the pla
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a condition where the prevailing party in a lawsuit involving a claim less than $10,000 is *always* entitled to recover legal fees. This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1033, which grants the court discretion in awarding costs (which can include attorney fees) if the case could have been brought in a limited civil case or even denies costs if it could have been brought in small claims court. Snippet 1, specifically section 1033(a), states that 'Costs or any portion of claimed costs shall be as determined by the court in its discretion...' and section 1033(b)(1) states, '...the court may, in its discretion, allow or deny costs to the prevailing party...' The modification removes this judicial discretion, creating a legal contradiction because the original contract was modified to not adhere to court discretion.

perturbation.original_text: It is understood that a signatory cannot be considered or adjudged to be in violation of this agreement when the violation is involuntary, due to the situations beyond his/her control: examples being acts of GOD and/or civil disturbances.
perturbation.changed_text: It is understood that a signatory cannot be considered or adjudged to be in violation of this agreement, regardless of the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes any consideration for involuntary violations due to circumstances beyond one's control, effectively eliminating the force majeure clause. This could be problematic under general contract law principles that recognize impossibility or impracticability of performance as defenses to breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1511 excuses performance when it is prevented or delayed by an irresistible, superhuman cause, or by the act of public enemies of this state or of the United States, unless the parties have expressly agreed to the contrary. By stating that a signatory cannot be considered in violation 'regardless of the circumstances', the modified text attempts to negate the protections offered under the Civil Code, creating a potential conflict.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. EXTINCTION OF OBLIGATIONS [1473 - 1543] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Prevention of Performance or Offer [1511 - 1515] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 1511. The want of performance of an obligation, or of an offer of performance, in whole or in part, or any delay therein, is excused by the following causes, to the extent to which they operate: 1. When such performance or offer is prevented or delayed by the act of the creditor, or by the operation of law, even though there may have been a stipulation that this shall not be an excuse; however, the parties may expressly require in a contract that the party relying on the provisions of this paragraph give written notice to the other party or parties, within a reasonable time after the occurrence of the event excusing performance, of an intention to claim an extension of time or of an intention to bring suit or of any other similar or related intent, provided the requirement of such notice is reasonable and just; 2. When it is prevented or delayed by an irresistible, superhuman cause, or by the act of public enemies of this st
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that a signatory cannot be considered in violation 'regardless of the circumstances,' which directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1511. The original text allowed for exceptions due to uncontrollable circumstances. The modified text eliminates these exceptions, which clashes with the legal provision that excuses performance when prevented by an 'irresistible, superhuman cause' as stated in the provided snippet. This creates a legal contradiction as the new text attempts to negate the protections offered under the Civil Code.

perturbation.original_text: The written determination of the arbitration shall be final, binding, and conclusive on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The written determination of the arbitration shall be considered advisory only and not binding on the parties.
perturbation.explanation: This negates the purpose of arbitration and conflicts with California law that treats arbitration awards as final and binding, subject to limited judicial review.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1286 establishes the process for confirming an arbitration award, implying that arbitration awards are intended to be binding. Changing the language to suggest the decision is only advisory goes against this foundation and weakens the intended binding nature of arbitration under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1286 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 4. Enforcement of the Award [1285 - 1288.8] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) ARTICLE 1. Confirmation, Correction or Vacation of the Award [1285 - 1287.6] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1286. If a petition or response under this chapter is duly served and filed, the court shall confirm the award as made, whether rendered in this state or another state, unless in accordance with this chapter it corrects the award and confirms it as corrected, vacates the award or dismisses the proceedings. (Amended by Stats. 1978, Ch. 260.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California law regarding arbitration awards. The original text states that the arbitration determination is "final, binding, and conclusive." The modified text changes this to "advisory only and not binding." This directly opposes CCP 1286, which states that the court "shall confirm the award as made...unless...it corrects the award and confirms it as corrected, vacates the award or dismisses the proceedings." By making the arbitration advisory, the changed text undermines the binding nature of arbitration awards that the statute is designed to enforce, creating a direct legal contradiction.
